source_file: [Day 12] Adventure Kit; 30 Days Lost in Space (English_ASR).srt
full_text: Good morning Explorer I've managed to stop the leak from yesterday but the alarm system is still totally busted I trust you've had your morning cup of Rocket Fuel because today is going to be a melody of learning we're going to make some noise literally we're introducing another piece of our Cosmic puzzle the passive buzzer now don't be fooled by its rather mundane name this little one can make quite a racket when it wants to it's a small speaker that can play different frequencies and we're going to use that ability to our advantage today's Mission should you choose to accept it is to turn our 4x4 keypad into an 8bit sounding piano keyboard that's right by the end of today your control panel will have its very own mini synth once we can verify that it's working we can rewire our alarm system how's that for turning it up to 11 so buckle in check your dials and let's hit the launch button on this day sound good I sure hope so let's talk about our new comrad and arms the passive buzzer this small usually cylindrical component is capable of generating a range of sound frequencies it's like a tiny speaker that you can program in the electrical Universe the passive buzzer is a piso electric device when an oscillating electrical signal is applied to its terminals it vibrates and creates sound we control the frequency of this oscillation which translates to the pitch of the sound that it generates to put it simply you give it a frequency and tell it how long to play it simple to accomplish this we can use pulse width modulation and it sings that note back to you so what do you say Explorer let's get ready to compose some Galactic Symphonies shall we now in order to make the passive buzzer produce any sound we need to send it some sort of frequency onto its signal pin in terms of music using a different frequency will produce a different sound it is not possible to generate tones lower than 31 Hertz and struggles to produce higher than 4,978 Herz if you want an optional additional Library it may be in your best interest to check out the tone H library that way if you want a specific note you can say notes 5 instead of some arbitrary number like 523 I've added a copy to your mission resources luckily sending those tones is made easy with a built-in function called tone tone takes two parameters the pin and the frequency you want to send optionally you can also put a third for duration now let's brainstorm together Explorer we want each button on our keypad to trigger a specific tone Oh I can hear you now let's just churn out a bunch of if statements one for each button if button one is pressed play 200 h Hertz if button a is pressed play 600 HZ and so forth a simplistic approach but not very elegant is it instead let's introduce some finesse into our coding Journey how about we conjure up a second two-dimensional array we'll Christen it as something suitable like tones a title befitting its role and then there you have it we only need one if statement this method is neater and easier to debug plus it's a great way to flex those Hefty programming muscles we've been developing let's continue shall we I want to take the time to introduce a new programming concept Loops I'm sure you already know about our Loop function but the loops we will be talking about today are used in nearly all programming languages universally and are in code everywhere what I'm referencing are called four and while Loops four Loops execute a block of code a number of times and are usually usually used if you or the code knows the number of times it should Loop a for Loop looks like this and declares the initial Loop counter value here a test of that counter and by how much the counter changes with each Loop in this example counter starts at zero and the loop will continue until counter is no longer less than 10 each time the loop runs I increases by one which is what i++ signifies once I is no longer less than 10 the loop is broken and the program moves on to the next line of code next let's move on to while Loops these are used when we don't know how many times the loop will need to run it just keeps running as long as a certain condition is true once that condition is no longer true the loop stops here's the format as long as condition is true the code inside the curly braces will run make sure to alter something within the loop to eventually break the condition or you'll end up with an endless loop a space Explorer's worst nightmare believe me you get all that allow me to give you a Snappy rundown then four Loops you know how many times you need to do a thing while Loops you don't know how many times you'll do a thing you just do it until a condition stops being true you've got this Explorer I'm about 94.8 63% sure of it fun little trick for you if you want to use it if you add while true at the end of setup you can halt your code there to debug the setup function you can technically do that wherever in your code to help debug before a specific point I suppose I've babbled on enough for now let's turn to some instructional material that I was able to gather cached on the computer I've also added this to your mission resources for future reference hello everybody and welcome back to day 12 of the 30 days loss in space code explanations we'll be taking the code we had yesterday from day 12 and adding a little bit more to it in order to add a little bit of functionality for our buzzer and adding the various tones for it so I'm sure as you remember yesterday going through most of yesterday's code we have honestly the majority of this at the top is very very similar to what we had before Define the keypad Define the row column arrays Define the 2D array to say what each button is and what character they represent and then Define the keypad and then at the end of that whole scenario we just waited until a button was pressed and we said this is a button that was pressed to make sure that our keypad was up and working properly now we're going to be taking that same code we had yesterday most of it being the same all the way up until here and then we're actually going to add in an additional piece of functionality that just simply says hey when you press this button in the top left of this uh keypad that we've defined I wanted to also play a tone a tone match for that specific key and so what I have here is I have another 2D array shortly below the original one and this is not of characters but of integers integers being the frequency represented as an interor value that we want to play the various tones on our buzzer now you might know or probably don't that the buzzer has a certain range of frequencies that it can actually play the lowest being 31 which is the lowest of the low so uh one thing to make note of is if it doesn't play perfectly um remember that that is the extreme like bottom range of it so sometimes it struggles to play the very very extreme ends of the of the spectrum of values that it can do so the lowest value can play is 31 and the highest value frequency in terms of Hertz that it can play is I believe 3729 which is this value here you'll notice if you try playing zero it shouldn't end up doing anything which is what would happen if you pressed essentially the D button it should do nothing just because it is currently outside of the ranges possible for it to play in this example will be setting the buzzer pin to pin 10 which should follow the wiring diagram along with the video down below next we have setup obviously I'm sure you guys have seen this 100 times we're just going to talk on the serial monitor at that b rate void Loop let's hop down here Char button character equals my awesome pad. Wa for key same as yesterday all it does is it will print simply oh actually it doesn't have the print statement but it just adds to this character whatever button you press so if you press the button the number five it'll have save character five as button character now let's talk about something new let's talk about the idea of what a for Loop is actually I'm sure you guys already know what Loop is loop here is essentially in a weird roundabout way you can think of it as just saying like while true or while one etc etc etc because Loop this will never stop going through this Loop over and over and over and over and over again but that's just something built into the uino IDE this is not to be confused with four loops and while Loops I think we might have talked about potentially while Loops or something similar in the past but let's go real quick and let's talk about the two different types of Loops right now those would be while Loops which will execute until something becomes false or that condition becomes false so if you have something like while true you know like while this is essentially saying this will never stop looping because this will never turn to false but if you say while but Buton character is is not equal to five well this will only execute while this is anything except for five so let's just say you type in six it's going to execute n it's going to execute but as soon as you press five ah it'll uh it'll say this is false and it'll jump past the while loop so that's how while loop works that's where you don't know how long you'll necessarily want it to go you know you don't have like a number like 10 or I'm going to go through this exactly five times so when you don't know those type of things a while loop is useful but what we're talking about today is a little bit different it's called a for Loop and this is where you do know exactly how long you want it to go through the loop so the for Loop is composed of three parts each part is the start where the operations is performed in the loop starts in this case we're going to say that by I is equal to zero this is essentially like the variable it's going to be checking each time you go through the loop and this variable will be changing each time you go for through the loop next you have a condition this is a condition that says are we actually going to go through this Loop or not so this checks this if this is false it skips it over just like the Y Loop finally you have the step now remember I said that this is what it's going to have initialized at the beginning of the loop this is how it's actually going to end up changing what the value in the loop is so in this case we know exactly how many times we need to go through the loop because we need to go through the loop exactly four times because that's how many rows we have so we can use a variable row since we know that's going to be four to go through exactly four times and in this for loop we're going exactly in through column times let me quickly explain to you what this Loop is doing first we're going to go through each of the rows four starting at index of zero we're going to go through the rows and we're going to check to see in the first row 0 1 2 3 do any of these match the button that was pressed again let's say that the button press was five let's just keep that consistent so every time it goes through it's going to check is this five is this five is this five is this five so we're going to go through each of the rows and then each of the columns and we're going to check is this equal to five and what until we hit five then we get a jump down to here then we go this turns to essentially one and then finally down below we say if button character is equal to the buttons i j meaning buttons of in this case it would be Row one column one so one one so like I said one one then we found it and we can set the tone frequency up here to be equal to what we ma this to in tones i j so we go tones 1 one is going to be set to 311 so we just we're just mapping the value from buttons to the tone here so that's all this is doing in this double four Loop next we're going to go down and we're going to print the following information to our console just for easy debugging purposes key and we're going to say what button we actually pressed and the frequency that it's mapped to that we were able to get from the two double nested for Loops finally we go to if tone frequency is greater than zero which should be assum you didn't press that very last button then tone buzzer pin a tone frequency and then we're going to play that tone at that frequency until stopped and the only way we'll have the stop tone is if we press a frequency of zero which is the way to stop it is again press that D button essentially in the bottom right corner you can think of that as our little stop key and that sets a no tone on the buzzer pin just telling us hey cut it out and with that that should be just about brought all the code for day 12 I also highly recommend before you quickly end this little section there is an extra little bonus lesson it's really really important and I highly highly recommend you check it out all you need to do is go to 12a copy the code and hit run uh and it's really really important I implore you to do so welcome to the day 12 wiring example video today we'll be using the output of our for 4x4 keypad to control the output of another device the new device that we'll be using today is a piso electric buzzer we have one already connected to our breadboard but uh we also have another one that is disconnected that we can inspect we can see that the buzzer has its leads labeled the leftand lead right here is lab labeled with a minus sign and that's for the ground connection the right hand lead is labeled with an S for signal and these will be the connections that we make to our hero in order to make the buzzer produce sound if we turn our attention to the breadboard we see that here the buzzer negative connection here on the left is connected to Hero ground by way of this handy ground bus that we've set up by way of this black jumper wire the uh positive lead that is the s Lead of the buzzer is connected through this jumper wire to one of the heroes pulse with modulated output pins in this case it's connected to pin number 10 which was otherwise free for us to use and our choice to use pin 10 to drive the buzzer is consistent with the programming that we've built in to our software sketch the keypad connections remain the same as they were last time on day 11 so we don't really need to uh discuss that too much now our sketch is designed to drive this buzzer to produce sound at a specified frequency that frequency is determined in our sketch by which key is pressed according to our sketch design the sound will continue until another key is pressed or it will turn off altogether if we press the lower right hand D key which actually commands a frequency of zero so that turns off the sound so we should go ahead now and upload the sketch and see if it functions as we expect when we do so we can select different buttons to choose the lowest frequencies should be produced by the lower numbered keys that is Keys towards the upper leftand corner of the keypad and then we'll get successively higher frequencies as we move to the right and down on the keypad so we'll try that out and hear the different frequencies that uh we have selected in writing our software sketch so we should also be aware that we can keep track of the key presses and the buzzer frequencies if while we're running the sketch we watch the serial Monitor and see the numbers that are reported there so we'll try to pay attention to all those things as we go ahead now and upload the sketch so we'll come over to the IDE sketch is loading now and we have the sketch running and so now if we press any of the keys we should hear a corresponding tone coming from our buzzer so for example if we press key five we should expect to generate a tone with a frequency of 311 Hertz and that'll continue until we press key D and that shuts it down and sure enough that's exactly what we see on the serial monitors key5 was pressed and produced a frequency of 311 and we can go through from low to high frequency for so for example the lowest frequency that we're programmed here to produce is 31 Hertz a low frequency buzz and then we can go up to higher and higher frequencies and anytime we want want we can shut it off with key D and we have the highest frequencies here so it appears that our buzzer circuit and the accompanying sketch seem to be working well you should have a little bit of fun experimenting with this seeing what you can do with it and next time we'll try putting this new capability that we've developed to some practical use excellent work today Explorer you've successfully orchestrated a symphony of beeps and bloops with your newly installed passive buzzer and keypad music to my artificial ears you're doing relatively well for a species that requires a prolonged period of time to rest in order to function you've got the hang of loops and you've even started controlling the pitch frequencies to create different sounds you're beginning to command this eight bit world like a maestro but as it should be abundantly clear by now we are far from done tomorrow we'll take this keypad buzzer Duo to a whole new level instead of just a melodic instrument we're transforming it into a high-tech security system a personal identification number is the game and you'll learn how to integrate it into your control panel rest well for day 13 comes with its own set of challenges will you rise to the occasion time will tell as for me I will be ready as I always am wait hold on a moment Explorer it seems like we've just received an unexpected data transmission it's absolutely uncanny how the unexpected always seems to pop up in the most inconvenient times for your sake I hope you're as Nimble with surprises as you are with coding I've deciphered the transmission and it appears to be code yes an Arduino sketch it could be an important piece on our ongoing Mission my digital intuition tells me you should run it on your HERO board here's how to proceed step one save any ongoing work this could get a bit disruptive step two clear your current sketch and replace it with the new code step three compile and upload the code to your board and remember Explorer always anticipate the unexpected when executing unknown code while the code is running I'll keep scanning for any related signals and of course I'll be here to guide you through any technical issues onward we go Explorer let's crack this code
